points.text: Add rate limiting/IP throttling on the login endpoint to curb brute‑force attempts and reduce DB load.
Implement exponential backoff and retry for transient DB lock errors in the authentication flow.
Optimize the DB: add indexes on user lookup fields, shorten transaction scope, and consider read replicas for login reads.
Enable account lockout after N failed attempts and deploy CAPTCHA to improve security.
Optimize the login DB path: add appropriate indexes, switch to row‑level or optimistic locking, and batch writes to reduce lock duration.
Add IP‑based rate limiting and exponential back‑off on failed logins to curb brute‑force attempts and lower load spikes.
Wrap the authentication service in a circuit breaker with a short timeout to isolate overload and protect downstream services.
Add an index on the column(s) used to check login credentials (e.g., username/email) to reduce lock time.
Move failed login attempts to a separate table and index by IP and timestamp to avoid contention on the main users table.
Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE only when necessary; prefer read‑only queries for authentication checks.
Reduce transaction scope around login logic; commit quickly to free locks.
Enable connection pooling (e.g., PgBouncer) to limit concurrent sessions during peaks.
Implement rate limiting or account lockout per IP to cut down malicious attempts.
Consider using READ COMMITTED isolation for login queries to avoid unnecessary row locks.
Add a covering index on the login identifier (e.g., username/email) to eliminate full‑table scans and shorten row locks during peak logins.
Limit each login transaction to the minimal reads/writes (fetch user, verify password) and commit immediately to reduce lock duration.
Use READ COMMITTED (or snapshot) isolation for login queries; avoid SELECT … FOR UPDATE unless truly needed.
Implement a lightweight login‑attempt counter (per IP or user) with an atomic UPDATE that increments a column and expires rows, enabling throttling without heav
Add rate limiting or login throttling per IP/account to curb brute‑force attempts and reduce DB load.
Implement exponential back‑off or temporary account lockout after repeated failed logins.
Introduce CAPTCHA after a few failed attempts to block automated attacks.
Cache password hash verification or switch to stateless JWT validation to minimize DB reads during login.
Use read‑replicas or a dedicated authentication database to alleviate lock contention under peak traffic.
Deploy adaptive rate limiting on login attempts per IP/user to cap bursts during peaks.
Detect credential stuffing by monitoring high failure rates across IPs and trigger step‑up verification (CAPTCHA or MFA).
Apply exponential backoff or progressive delays for repeated failed logins to lower DB lock contention.
Move authentication checks to a lightweight cache or dedicated auth service to reduce DB load during traffic spikes.
